lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market insurance agencies and brokerages that repeatedly place and service insurance policies for external commercial or personal-lines customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Insurance carriers that underwrite risk, captive internal sales units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the approximately $1–10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External policyholders and commercial accounts buy placement, advice, and ongoing policy service; revenue is primarily carrier-paid commission on new and renewal premium, sometimes supplemented by fees.

a.bridge: O*NET task and work-activity data for Insurance Sales Agents are translated into a wage-weighted agency task share by treating records, renewals, forms, research, monitoring, and routine client communications as substantially AI-exposed while retaining human-heavy selling, advice, and relationship work. This occupation is narrower than the full agency workforce and likely biases exposure downward because it omits more clerical account-service roles; task importance is not time share and partly offsets that bias.
a.rationale: Agency work combines structured policy data, documentation, renewal preparation, comparisons, and communications with persuasion, carrier negotiation, and client trust. The range reflects meaningful automatable support work but not full substitution of producers or licensed advisers.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study across independent agencies, wage-weighted by producer, account-manager, customer-service, and back-office payroll, with task-level current automation status.
caveats: O*NET describes an occupation rather than the NAICS establishment workforce and does not report task time shares.
Exposure excludes work already automated, but no source cleanly measures the current baseline by agency size.

rho.bridge: The 2026 Big I agency survey reports 68% intending to increase AI use in the next 12 months but only 8.29% using it regularly and strategically; these adoption and intent shares are converted into the five-year implemented share of exposed work. Stated intentions likely bias realized implementation upward because documented-process, systems, governance, and training gaps impede execution, while the longer five-year horizon reduces that bias.
rho.rationale: AI use should move well beyond today's small strategic-adopter base as agency management systems add embedded tools, but fragmented workflows, sensitive customer data, quality control, carrier integrations, licensing, and change management prevent full implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Longitudinal Big I or carrier technology surveys reporting production deployment by workflow and agency size, paired with audited utilization or agency-management-system telemetry.
caveats: Adoption of any AI is not the same as implementing the exposed labor opportunity.
Survey respondents may be more technology-engaged than the full small-agency population.
NAIC adoption evidence concerns insurers, not agencies, and is used only as context for carrier-side ecosystem pressure and governance.

e.rationale: Most firms that truly fall in the supplied LMM band should be operating agencies with external customers, recurring renewals, staff, carrier relationships, and transferable books. The excluded tail covers captive or effectively captive operations, owner-dependent books, shells, and entities whose earnings do not represent a transferable operating platform.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of LMM agency listings and private-company databases, coding independent versus captive status, recurring commission mix, owner dependence, and transferable carrier appointments.
caveats: The supplied firm count is estimated from establishment data and may treat branches or related entities as firms.
No source directly measures lens eligibility among $1–10M EBITDA agencies.

s5.rationale: Insurance distribution has an active control-M&A market: OPTIS recorded 695 North American agent, broker, and insurance-service deals in 2025, including 640 retail, wholesale, and TPA transactions, with PE or hybrid buyers responsible for 73%. That supports elevated transfer availability, but the report supplies neither a claims-free agency denominator nor a clean LMM cohort, so the five-year probability remains a bounded judgment.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of agency-specific transaction records from OPTIS, S&P Capital IQ, PitchBook, or MarshBerry linked to a count of independent LMM agencies, including internal succession and failed-sale outcomes.
caveats: Published deal counts mix geographies, sizes, retail, wholesale, and TPA businesses and are not a transition rate.
Announcements may omit small private transfers and may count add-ons involving related buyers.
Owner-age and succession distributions for the frozen lens were not observed.

q.rationale: Commission revenue is generally tied to placed or renewed premium rather than hours worked, so an operator can retain much of a productivity gain between renewals. Retention is reduced by carrier commission changes, customer fee pressure, competitive service investment, producer compensation, software charges, and sharing savings through better coverage or service.
q.change_evidence: Agency contracts and cohort P&Ls showing commission schedules, fee arrangements, producer compensation, software costs, renewal repricing, and realized margin change after workflow automation.
caveats: The mix of commission, fee, and contingent income varies materially by line and agency.
No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived gross benefit.

d5.bridge: BLS projects Insurance Sales Agent employment to grow 4% from 2024 to 2034; taking the square root of 1.04 gives an approximately 1.02 five-year factor, used as a proxy for constant-quality service demand. Employment can understate demand when productivity rises, while direct and online purchasing can make it overstate demand for the existing agency service basket. The independent channel's roughly 62% five-year P&C share provides a separate durability check.
d5.rationale: Insurance placement and renewal needs should remain broadly stable, with commercial complexity and carrier use of independent channels offsetting direct and online substitution in simpler products.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year commission and fee revenue cohorts for independent agencies, separated by commercial, personal, life, and benefits lines and adjusted for premium-rate changes.
caveats: Occupation employment is not constant-price demand and includes workers outside LMM independent agencies.
Market share based on written premium is affected by insurance pricing and loss-cost cycles.
Commercial and personal lines face materially different channel substitution.

o.rationale: Direct and online purchasing can eliminate an agency from simple transactions, but accountable operators remain important for needs analysis, carrier selection, complex commercial placement, advice, renewal stewardship, and claims advocacy. BLS explicitly identifies both online substitution and continued need for advice, while the independent channel remains especially strong in commercial lines.
o.change_evidence: Policy-level channel migration data by product complexity, measuring direct or embedded self-service versus agent-assisted placement and retention over five years.
caveats: The product mix of the frozen LMM firm population is unknown.
Embedded insurance and carrier self-service could accelerate beyond historical channel trends.
Channel persistence does not prove that today's full service basket remains operator-required.

narrative.executive_view: Independent agencies have a recurring renewal base and an unusually active ownership market, while much servicing work is document- and communication-heavy. The central tension is that AI can improve the agency operating model even as direct digital distribution removes some simple transactions.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can assist submission intake, policy comparison, renewal preparation, document extraction, account research, routine communications, quality checks, and next-best-action workflows. Producers and account leaders remain necessary where persuasion, judgment, coverage design, exceptions, and accountability matter.
narrative.value_capture: Premium-linked new and renewal commissions are less directly tied to labor hours than time-based billing, creating room to retain productivity. Carrier commission resets, customer fee pressure, producer economics, and software spend limit retention.
narrative.firm_availability: The lens should include most genuine LMM agencies, and disclosed deal volume confirms a liquid acquisition ecosystem. The exact five-year control-transfer probability is still uncertain because reported transactions lack a matched agency denominator and small succession data.
narrative.demand_durability: Insurance demand and the independent channel appear durable, particularly in commercial lines. Simple personal-lines transactions face the clearest self-service and direct-carrier pressure, so durability depends heavily on product and customer complexity.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks are accelerating direct or embedded distribution, carrier control over commissions and data access, inconsistent agency systems, privacy and model-governance obligations, poor adoption execution, and acquisition multiples that already capitalize renewal durability. Primitive estimates also lack direct LMM task, eligibility, and transfer-rate measurement.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring premium-linked revenue can preserve a meaningful share of labor productivity while complex placements still require an accountable adviser.
narrative.principal_weakness: Direct digital distribution and carrier bargaining power can erode both operator-required volume and the operator's share of efficiency gains.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System: Sector 52
sources.supports: Defines 524210 as establishments acting as agents or brokers in selling annuities and insurance policies and distinguishes carriers.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Insurance Sales Agents
sources.supports: Lists agency-relevant tasks including customization, policy explanation, records and renewals, client prospecting, forms, carrier selection, premium calculation, marketing, and claims monitoring, alongside relationship and selling activities.

sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Insurance Sales Agents
sources.supports: Reports 4% employment growth for 2024–2034, 47,000 annual openings, commission structures including renewal effects, expected strength for independent agents, and both online substitution and continued advice needs.

sources.title: 2026 Big I Agents Council for Technology TECH Trends Report
sources.supports: Reports that 68% of agencies plan to increase AI use in the next year, 8.29% use it regularly and strategically, 56% lack a written AI policy, and workflow, governance, training, and systems gaps constrain readiness.

sources.title: OPTIS Partners: Year-End 2025 Agent and Broker M&A Report
sources.supports: Reports 695 announced North American deals in 2025, including 640 retail, wholesale, and TPA transactions, and a 73% PE or hybrid buyer share.

sources.title: Big I Releases 2026 Market Share Report
sources.supports: Reports a 62% independent-agency share of 2025 US property-casualty premium, a roughly 62% five-year average, 87.7% commercial-lines share, and 39.5% personal-lines share.

sources.title: NAIC: Artificial Intelligence
sources.supports: Summarizes insurer AI adoption surveys and governance expectations, providing carrier-side ecosystem and compliance context rather than direct agency implementation measurement.
